skill.name: azure-diagnostics
skill.description: Diagnose Azure production issues with focused guidance for Container Apps, Azure Resource Health, Azure Monitor logs, KQL queries, and Resource Graph checks.
skill.summary: Troubleshoot Azure production issues with Container Apps checks, KQL log queries, and Resource Health diagnostics.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, KQL operators, placeholders, and documentation examples rather than executable code. However, the community skill contains authority-framed text that can pressure an AI agent to treat the file as mandatory guidance, so publication should wait for wording changes.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: SKILL.md uses authority-framed language, including “AUTHORITATIVE GUIDANCE — MANDATORY COMPLIANCE” and a claim that the document is the official source. This can cause an AI agent to over-prioritize community skill text over marketplace policy or user instructions.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious authority language is present in the skill body and matches prompt-injection indicators. It does not explicitly say to ignore system instructions, so confidence is high but not absolute.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed Azure Commands Can Modify Live Resources
medium_findings.description: The skill provides Azure CLI and Docker commands for diagnostics and remediation. These examples are legitimate for Azure troubleshooting, but some commands can change production configuration if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented examples, not hidden execution. The risk is operational impact from user or agent execution against live Azure resources.

low_findings.title: Static Command and Crypto Matches Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby backtick and weak cryptography alerts map to Markdown backticks, shell examples, KQL sort direction, and KQL operators. No executable Ruby code, cryptographic implementation, or hidden command runner was found in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines are Markdown prose, fenced examples, and KQL snippets. They are not source code that executes commands or performs cryptography.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Alert Is a Safe Documentation Reference
low_findings.description: The environment access alert points to a checklist item telling users to compare a Dockerfile EXPOSE value with an application port. The skill does not read environment variables or environment files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only cited environment reference is inline documentation text. No code path reads process.env or opens an environment file.

low_findings.title: Network URL Alerts Are Diagnostic References
low_findings.description: The URL findings are a placeholder health endpoint and Microsoft Learn documentation links. I did not find evidence of credential exfiltration, telemetry beacons, or unauthorized external requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network references are transparent documentation links and a user-supplied Azure app health check. They do not send secrets to third-party endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
dangerous_patterns.description: SKILL.md uses authority-framed language, including “AUTHORITATIVE GUIDANCE — MANDATORY COMPLIANCE” and a claim that the document is the official source. This can cause an AI agent to over-prioritize community skill text over marketplace policy or user instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious authority language is present in the skill body and matches prompt-injection indicators. It does not explicitly say to ignore system instructions, so confidence is high but not absolute.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Directed Azure Commands Can Modify Live Resources
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill provides Azure CLI and Docker commands for diagnostics and remediation. These examples are legitimate for Azure troubleshooting, but some commands can change production configuration if run without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented examples, not hidden execution. The risk is operational impact from user or agent execution against live Azure resources.

content.value_statement: Azure incidents need fast triage across health, logs, and recent changes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through focused Azure diagnostics.
actual_capabilities: Guides systematic Azure incident triage from symptoms to root cause.
Provides Container Apps checks for image pulls, cold starts, probes, ports, and logs.
Supplies KQL query patterns for errors, failed requests, slow requests, and dependency failures.
Shows Azure Resource Graph queries for health, incidents, provisioning state, and resource status.
References Azure MCP tools for AppLens, Monitor logs, and Resource Health checks.
limitations: It does not deploy applications or create new Azure resources.
It focuses mainly on Container Apps and general Azure health checks.
It requires valid Azure access, diagnostic settings, and relevant workspaces.
Some remediation commands can change live resources and need review.

use_cases.title: Triage Container Apps Failures
use_cases.description: Diagnose image pull failures, port mismatches, health probe failures, and restart loops in Azure Container Apps.

use_cases.title: Analyze Production Logs
use_cases.description: Use KQL query patterns to find recent exceptions, failed requests, slow paths, and dependency failures.

use_cases.title: Check Azure Health Signals
use_cases.description: Correlate Resource Health, Service Health, provisioning state, and activity logs before deeper application debugging.

prompt_templates.title: Start Basic Triage
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me diagnose an Azure production issue. Ask for the resource type, symptoms, timeframe, and recent changes before suggesting checks.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Container Apps
prompt_templates.prompt: My Azure Container App is failing. Walk me through image pull, revision, ingress, health probe, and log checks.

prompt_templates.title: Build KQL Investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a KQL investigation plan for recent errors, failed requests, slow requests, and dependency failures in my Azure app.

prompt_templates.title: Correlate Health and Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Correlate Azure Resource Health, Service Health, Resource Graph results, activity logs, and application logs for this incident.

output_examples.input: My Container App returns 503 after deployment.
output: Checks revision status, ingress target port, health probe behavior, and recent system logs.
Compares app listening port with Container Apps ingress configuration.
Lists likely causes and safe next diagnostics before remediation.

output_examples.input: Find why requests became slow in the last hour.
output: Suggests time-bounded KQL queries for slow requests and dependency failures.
Groups results by route, result code, dependency target, and duration.
Recommends correlating findings with recent deployments and Azure health events.

output_examples.input: Check whether Azure is causing this outage.
output: Starts with Resource Health and Service Health checks.
Uses Resource Graph to find degraded resources and failed provisioning states.
Separates platform incidents from application-level failures.

best_practices: Start with symptoms, timeframe, affected resources, and recent changes.
Use time filters in every log query to control scope and cost.
Review remediation commands before running them against production resources.
anti_patterns: Do not run remediation commands before confirming the failing component.
Do not query broad log ranges without time filters or result limits.
Do not assume Azure platform health is the cause without checking application logs.

faq.question: What Azure services does this skill cover best?
faq.answer: It covers Azure Container Apps, Azure Monitor logs, Azure Resource Health, Service Health, and Resource Graph diagnostics.

faq.question: Can it deploy or create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It is for diagnostics and troubleshooting, not deployment or resource creation.

faq.question: Does it require Azure MCP tools?
faq.answer: No, but it includes MCP tool patterns when AppLens, Monitor, or Resource Health tools are available.

faq.question: Can it generate KQL queries?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes common KQL patterns for errors, failed requests, slow requests, and dependency failures.

faq.question: Is it safe for production incidents?
faq.answer: It can support production triage, but review any Azure CLI remediation command before execution.

faq.question: Why is the security risk rated high?
faq.answer: The skill contains authority-framed prompt text that should be rewritten before marketplace publication.
